notes: Holmes traces the Blue Carbuncle through a chain of chance to Ryder, confronting him at 221B with a deduction so precise that Ryder collapses and confesses. Holmes then releases him without notifying the police — reasoning that a weak man driven to crime by opportunity is better reformed by fear than by prosecution, and that jailing him would only make him worse. Ryder vanishes, John Horner is exonerated, and Holmes considers the matter resolved. The scene is one of the clearest illustrations in the canon of Holmes's personal system of justice, which places reformation above punishment and operates entirely outside the courts.
